current_fact.fact_text: On 26 April 1991, a massive tornado outbreak ripped across the central United States, spawning 55 tornadoes in one day. The most intense, an F5 tornado near Andover, Kansas, carved a path of near-total destruction—yet also produced some of the clearest video ever recorded of a monster tornado at close range.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

current_fact.match_reason: The violent, crackling energy of an F5 tornado echoes the high-voltage rock in 'Electric Shock,' turning atmospheric electricity into guitar-driven turbulence.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1937, the Basque town of Guernica was bombed by German and Italian warplanes testing tactics for the looming world war. The attack obliterated much of the town and later inspired Pablo Picasso’s monumental painting 'Guernica,' which turned a local tragedy into a universal anti-war symbol.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: A defining trauma in Spain’s history connects with an episode tracing flamenco’s emotional depth—a tradition that often channels grief, resistance, and memory through music.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1945, near the very end of World War II, German forces launched the Battle of Bautzen—one of their last successful armored offensives. It produced a grim paradox: a tactical victory for a regime that was collapsing on every other front and only days away from surrender.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A late, hollow victory at the end of a collapsing era invites reflective distance; this episode looks back on pain and transition with the same slow, cinematic perspective.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1944, Georgios Papandreou became head of the Greek government-in-exile based in Egypt, representing a country occupied and fragmented by war. His government tried to balance Allied diplomacy, internal resistance movements, and the question of what Greece should look like after liberation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A Greek government displaced across the Mediterranean fits an episode sailing musically around that same sea, linking exile, coasts, and shared waters.

queue.fact_text: Also on 26 April 1944, Allied commandos in occupied Crete kidnapped German General Heinrich Kreipe in a daring night operation. They spirited him across rugged mountains and onto a boat, an almost cinematic mission that was so improbable it later inspired films and novels.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A mountain trek across occupied Crete to smuggle out a general aligns with an episode about mountainous landscapes and the stories that unfold among peaks and passes.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1954, the Geneva Conference opened in Switzerland, bringing Cold War powers together to negotiate over the future of Indochina and Korea. Around the table, diplomats tried to redraw maps and end wars with words instead of weapons—though the agreements they forged were fragile and often short-lived.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A conference gathering many actors to negotiate a complex peace mirrors a format that gathers many selectors and moods into one flowing sonic diplomatic table.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1923, Prince Albert, Duke of York (the future George VI), married Lady Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon at Westminster Abbey. Elizabeth reportedly had to be asked twice before she accepted his proposal, worried about losing her privacy—yet she went on to become the much-loved 'Queen Mother' and a symbol of wartime resilience.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: A royal wedding shaped by hesitation, vulnerability, and enduring partnership pairs perfectly with an episode explicitly devoted to love’s fragile, irrational side.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1964, the mainland nation of Tanganyika and the island of Zanzibar officially united to form the United Republic of Tanzania. The merger stitched together very different histories—Swahili coast trade networks, island sultanates, and mainland anti-colonial struggle—into a new experiment in shared identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: The birth of Tanzania, an East African union, connects with an episode celebrating African musical movements that likewise fuse diverse roots into a new, shared rhythm.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1986, a late-night safety test at the Chernobyl nuclear power plant in the Ukrainian SSR spiraled out of control, triggering a reactor explosion. The accident released vast amounts of radiation, forcing entire towns to be abandoned and leaving a long-lived 'exclusion zone' where nature has unexpectedly reclaimed human spaces.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: Chernobyl is a catastrophic example of technological overconfidence; this episode meditates on imperfection, unintended outcomes, and the uneasy beauty that can arise when things don’t go to plan.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1994, South Africans began voting in the country’s first multiracial general election. Lines of people stretched for kilometers as citizens who had never been allowed to vote before waited patiently for hours to help end apartheid and elect Nelson Mandela as president.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A foundational, hopeful 'first' for a nation matches Sedna’s own first episode—both signal the start of a new era, charged with political, cultural, and musical energy from Africa and beyond.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1945, the same day as battles raged elsewhere, the Greek city of Guernica’s wartime echoes were still traveling through Europe as Picasso’s painting toured. By then, 'Guernica' had already become a kind of visual protest song, so powerful that Franco’s regime reportedly tried to keep reproductions out of Spain.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

queue.match_reason: Picasso’s 'Guernica' turned horror into a shared symbolic space of resistance, much as this episode treats the dancefloor as a space where bodies and voices gather to reclaim power.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 2025, a vehicle ramming attack struck a Lapu-Lapu Day festival in Vancouver, killing 11 and injuring dozens. The event, meant to celebrate a Filipino hero who resisted Spanish colonization, was violently interrupted—another reminder of how public joy and public space can be shattered in a moment.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A jarring act of violence cutting through a civic celebration is paired with a calm, cinematic episode whose mood offers a kind of emotional decompression and quiet space after the shock.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1962, as satellites and probes left Earth, the idea of 'emptiness' in space quietly began to change. Experiments aboard those early missions revealed that what looked like a void is actually buzzing with particles, radiation, and invisible forces shaping everything from auroras to satellite orbits.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: Reframing 'empty' outer space as full of unseen activity echoes an episode that reframes disconnection and digital silence as a rich, mentally active space rather than a void.

queue.fact_text: On 26 April 1966, reconstruction plans for shattered Tashkent were drawn up that would ultimately turn it into a showcase Soviet city, with broad avenues and monumental modernist buildings. The disaster became an unexpected laboratory for urban design, testing how quickly an entire capital could be redesigned almost from scratch.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: Rebuilding a city by design parallels deliberately rebuilding our listening habits; both reject randomness in favor of careful, intentional choices about how space—urban or sonic—is used.

queue.fact_text: Across all these 26 April events—from meteor showers and moon impacts to quakes, elections, and experiments—runs a quiet thread: our planet and our societies are constantly being tested and reshaped. History on this date reminds us that change can fall from the sky, rise from the ground, or grow slowly out of people’s choices.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: A reflective, synthesis-style fact tying together all the day’s transformations fits with an episode about quiet shifts in perspective, movement, and noticing change as a kind of meditation.

published.fact_text: In 1962, NASA's Ranger 4 became the first American spacecraft to reach the Moon’s surface—by accident. A malfunction left it silent and uncontrollable, so it slammed into the far side of the Moon without sending back the close-up images it was built to capture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A Moon impact that produced 'emptiness' instead of data pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space and forward motion.

published.fact_text: Also on 26 April 1962, the United Kingdom launched Ariel 1, its very first satellite, making it the third country to place a satellite in orbit. Ironically, it was seriously damaged just months later by a high-altitude U.S. nuclear test, an early lesson in how human activity can clutter and endanger near-Earth space.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

published.match_reason: Ariel 1 marked Britain’s sonic leap into orbit; 'Destination Sedna' is framed as a collective voyage into new territories, mirroring that exploratory, outward-looking spirit.

published.fact_text: On 26 April 1993, Space Shuttle Columbia lifted off on mission STS-55, carrying the Spacelab laboratory in its cargo bay. For ten days, astronauts turned the shuttle into a flying science lab, running over 80 experiments that ranged from crystal growth to how living cells behave in weightlessness.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: STS-55 was a modular lab in orbit, experimenting with how things behave in new conditions, much like this episode maps experimental trip-hop labs that reshaped musical gravity.

published.fact_text: In 1803, the skies over the French town of L'Aigle erupted into a shower of thousands of stones. This meteorite fall was so dramatic and well-documented that it finally convinced many skeptical European scientists that rocks really can fall from the sky.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A sudden celestial 'rain' transforming what people believed about the sky parallels a mix that unfolds from mysterious calm into a storm of global rhythms, reshaping how we hear the world.

published.fact_text: On 26 April 1805, in the First Barbary War, U.S. Marines and mercenaries captured the Libyan city of Derne after a grueling desert march. It was the first time the American flag was raised in victory on foreign soil, an event immortalized in the Marine Corps anthem’s line “to the shores of Tripoli.”

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: An 1805 amphibious march and coastal siege resonates with an episode built around sea voyages, boats, and the emotional tides of traveling with others.

published.fact_text: In 1802, Napoleon Bonaparte signed a sweeping amnesty that allowed most émigrés of the French Revolution to return home. It was both a political calculation and a social experiment in bringing back thousands who had fled, trying to stitch a fractured nation back together.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

published.match_reason: An amnesty that invites exiles back mirrors an episode centered on reunion, homecoming, and the comfort of being under one roof again.

published.fact_text: The Battle of Beaumont on 26 April 1794 was a lesser-known clash of the War of the First Coalition, where French revolutionary forces defeated a coalition army in the Low Countries. Beneath the shifting battle lines was a larger experiment: could a radical new republic survive in a Europe dominated by monarchies?

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A revolutionary-era battle over political systems fits an episode about musical revolt, protest, and standing against entrenched power.

published.fact_text: On 26 April 1721, a devastating earthquake struck the Iranian city of Tabriz, leveling much of the city and killing thousands. Tabriz sits near several active faults, and over the centuries its architecture and urban layout have been repeatedly reshaped by the ground beneath it.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden seismic jolt that redraws a city connects with an episode themed around shocks and high-voltage energy, translating tectonic force into musical electricity.

published.fact_text: On 26 April 1966, a magnitude 5.1 earthquake struck Tashkent, then the largest city in Soviet Central Asia. Despite its moderate size, the shallow quake devastated the city, leaving hundreds of thousands homeless and turning Tashkent into a vast, long-term reconstruction project.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A city shaken and slowly rebuilt aligns with an episode about enduring storms, transitions, and the hope that destruction is not the final word.

published.fact_text: On 26 April 1991, a massive tornado outbreak ripped across the central United States, spawning 55 tornadoes in one day. The most intense, an F5 tornado near Andover, Kansas, carved a path of near-total destruction—yet also produced some of the clearest video ever recorded of a monster tornado at close range.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The violent, crackling energy of an F5 tornado echoes the high-voltage rock in 'Electric Shock,' turning atmospheric electricity into guitar-driven turbulence.
